text: A large fibroid, approximately one centimeter in length, is visible occupying the entire fundic region of the pelvis.
The surgeon begins the myomectomy procedure by achieving vascular control of the uterus, aiming to clip the uterine arteries at their origin to minimize hemorrhagic risk.
The surgeon encounters difficulty accessing the pedicle due to the large fibroid occupying the pelvic region, obstructing the surgical field.
The surgeon allows the insufflated gas to expand the surgical field, creating space for dissection within the pelvic region occupied by the fibrome.
The surgeon encounters difficulty as the voluminous fibrome obstructs access to the external iliac vessels, with the artery remaining deep and challenging to expose.
An assistant pulls on the trunk and the heterovary ligament to expose the area, while the surgeon attempts to locate and clip the uterine artery at its origin.
The surgeon attempts to increase the mobility of the uterus by sectioning the upper ligament after unsuccessful efforts to locate the artery, with the fibroid obstructing access.
After completing the final section, the surgical team increases exposure to reveal the umbilical artery at the parietal level, preparing to trace and identify the targeted artery.
The assistant applies traction on the upper ligament, providing easier access to the retroperitoneal area for dissection.
The surgeon exposes the two iliac vessels, including the artery and vein, and identifies the umbilical artery below them, which is now clearly visible after being elevated.
The surgeon follows the identified artery, searching for a vessel that detaches from it. The vessel is then dissected from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery, which is visibly pulsating, and prepares to clip it after increasing the exposure of the surgical field.
The assistant pushes the uterus to the left, allowing the surgeon to open the retroperitoneal space. The round ligament is carefully cut to quickly locate the umbilical artery.
The surgeon uses one hand to maneuver around the large fibroid, attempting to grasp the round ligament to expand the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon grasps the round ligament and pulls it to increase exposure of the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery within the opened retroperitoneal space, following the incision of the round ligament.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery by its pulsating nature, visualizes the uterus, and proceeds to develop the pararectal and paravesical fossae. The artery is then clipped without delay.
The surgeon clips the right uterine artery, securing it within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the intervention using bipolar energy to make a single incision, carefully coagulating the entire capsule until reaching the fibroma plane.
The surgeon highlights the large volume of the fibroma, noting the minimal hemorrhagic nature of the surgical field at this stage.
The surgeon removes the capsule surrounding the fibroma, allowing the assistant to insert an instrument between the fibroma and the capsule to irrigate the area with water.
The surgeon performs hydro dissection, introducing fluid between the fibroma and the capsule to facilitate separation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses hydro dissection to separate the fibroma from its myometrial attachments, visibly detaching the fibroma from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The assistant uses hydro dissection to push water between the fibroma and surrounding tissues, while the surgeon coagulates the adjacent tissues to control bleeding.
The assistant pushes fluid between the fibroma and the uterus, while the surgeon pulls on the fibroma to gradually identify and separate the tissue planes.
The assistant uses hydro dissection, spreading approximately four to five centimeters below the fibroma, to inject water and separate the mioma mass from the myometrial tissue.
The LED device is used to assist in applying traction to the fibroma, aiding in its separation from the myometrial attachments.
The surgeon positions the helioscopic clamp close to the LED to enhance traction force after identifying the tissue plane between the fibroid and the myometrium.
After completing the hydrodissection, the surgeon does not perform hemostasis on the pericapsular vessels, indicating that these vessels are not actively bleeding.
The LED device pushes water into the surgical field, aiding in the separation of the fibroid mass from the surrounding myometrial tissue.
The surgeon uses a coagulation device to cauterize the well-visualized vessels, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon applies traction in multiple directions, revealing large vessels under tension that require coagulation to prevent bleeding and maintain a clear surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the gliding plane between the fibroma and the healthy myometrium, with visible liquid aiding in the separation of these tissue layers.
The surgeon regulates the pericapsular vessels, ensuring they are well controlled, while maintaining a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the scolioscopic position, pinching close to the targeted area to optimize the strength and precision of the surgical maneuver.
The surgeon uses water to enhance the dissection plane, gradually nucleating the fibroid from the surrounding healthy myometrium.
A vessel is identified near the uterus and is being coagulated to control bleeding.
The trocars are positioned higher than usual, approximately six to eight centimeters above the umbilical cord, indicating an altered approach for this myomectomy procedure.
The fibroma is more than fifty percent enucleated, and the myoma mass is being pushed back.
In the left hypochondriac region, the surgeon coagulates the remaining fibroma tissue before sectioning it to desolidate it from its myometrial attachment.
The surgical field shows a small remaining portion of fibroma attached to the uterus, following previous enucleation and coagulation steps.
The fibroma is visibly pushed into the left hypochondriac region, occupying the entire space due to its volume.
The surgical field shows a mixture of red blood and clear serum fluid surrounding the fibroma during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon clips the uterine arteries to minimize bleeding during the procedure, ensuring a clearer surgical field.

The fibroma is positioned in the left hypochondriac region. The surgeon uses a Vicryl-I suture with a needle number forty-five to quickly approximate the edges of the uterine tissue, aiming to achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon moves approximately three to four centimeters on each side to invaginate the ithyrus berges, ensuring proper alignment and positioning of the tissue.
The surgeon invaginates the tissue at the point of incision, preparing to achieve rapid hemostasis using trachoporal or intracorporeal sutures.
The surgeon performs rapid suturing on the uterus to achieve hemostasis, as the uterine arteries begin to revascularize following temporary clippage.
The surgeon performs quick intracorporeal sutures to invaginate the tissues inside the uterus, ensuring good quality sutures to achieve effective hemostasis.
The surgeon passes the suture point, causing the tissue to invaginate into the uterine wall, with the tendons visibly accommodating the invaginated tissue.
The surgeon pulls the uterus from one end to the other at the bottom of the crater to achieve effective hemostasis.
The surgeon places sutures parallel to the hystereotomy incision on the uterus, ensuring precise alignment for effective tissue closure.
The surgeon tightens the suture point, creating a secure knot that appears as if two knots have been made, enhancing the stability of the closure.
The assistant invaginates the uterus, manipulating it to facilitate the surgical procedure.
The surgeon rapidly places a stitch along the hystereotomy site using a suture material, ensuring the stitch is secure without breaking the thread.
The surgeon uses the intracorporeal knotting technique to secure sutures within the surgical field.
The assistant invaginates the uterine tissue, folding it inward to facilitate the surgical procedure.
The surgeon tightens the suture around the uterus, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The surgeon completes the knot, ensuring the tissues are tightly skeined together, and prepares to place additional sutures as needed.
The surgeon is in the final stages of completing the myomectomy and suturing the fibroma, ensuring the surgical site is secure and properly closed.
The assistant pushes the tissue while the surgeon pulls firmly, applying tension to the sutured area.
The surgeon observes the invagination of tissues, indicating improved structural alignment and healing progress.
The surgical field is inspected for crested areas, which could lead to post-operative adhesion, but none are visible.
The surgeon completes the suture of the fibroma following the myomectomy procedure, with the sutured area visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places large fibromas into an endoscopic bag with a capacity of approximately 1200 cc, preparing them for removal.
